sentences.text: He took us into his home, his house.
He lived with his wife and sister-in-law, who became my stepmother a few years later.
His wife and-- and sister were a second cousin to my mother.
Excuse me.
And as soon as my sister recovered from the-- the typhus, we got on the train and went back to Debrecen, to see what we can find, or if we can find my brother.
Well, the liberation was in 1945, around Easter and Pesach time.
Yeah.
That was the week of Pesach.
Because my mother died the last day of Pesach.
I have yahrzeit on the same day, all the time, 1945.
We went back to Debrecen to-- to see if we can find my brother.
And to-- the war-- being that the war was still on, we couldn't write to my father.
And we came back.
We found our apartment in a ghetto, empty.
And I found a lot of pictures, a lot of personal-- personal things that I gathered together, that you will see later.
It was scattered on the floor.
It was-- it was strewn out of the drawers.
No, but there were no furniture left.
Furnitures were taken.
They just threw it-- scattered it on the floor.
It was left there.
That's all.
Nobody lived in the apartment.
Whatever-- whatever other belongings, furniture there, they took.
That was taken.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, I found my cousin to my father, who was hiding in Russia.
He was the police chief of Debrecen.
He became, on his own, because he was in cahoots with the Russians.
And I went to him for help.
Because the apartment that I wanted, the Hungarians won't let me in.
And he sent the Russian soldiers to see to it that they put the people out and I-- I go in.
And then we try to just survive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Go to the railroad station every day to see who's coming in and people that came.
We asked for my brother and some-- some of them said that my brother died in Auschwitz or in Mauthausen, that they were together.
And six months went by.
And my brother walked in.
Yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I-- I-- I was one a-- on a Main Street.
And somebody came over telling me, I saw your brother come home.
And I hit him.
I was so furious that he were-- I thought he was playing a joke on me.
And he repeated to me, go home.
He came home.
[CRYING]
Cried.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
We fell on each others' arm and cried.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because he didn't know we lost our mother until he walked into the house.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] After that, we became very bitter.
We wanted to take out our frustration on the Gentiles.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If somebody looked at me the wrong way I was ready to kill them.
I got into fights, furious fights, where-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Where my father was warned that if I don't-- you know, I don't calm down, they're not going to let me out of the country.
Somebody wrote to my father.
And my father wrote a letter, that I should take it easy.
Because they-- they're not going to let me out.
I never-- I never read the letter.
I was very, very bitter.
And so was my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then things got a little-- little better.
I drove a truck for American Joint Distribution.
Because they-- they needed somebody that didn't need wages.
Because--
My-- in Debrecen.
Because my father was in America.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I drove to Budapest and back, and the-- and the smaller towns in Debrecen, around Debrecen, delivering food packages.
You mean Debrecen?
My father sent us money.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I smuggled people out of Hungary, on a-- on a truck to Israel, into Austria.
As foreign workers with phony papers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I wanted to go to Israel.
But being that my father was in America, I had to come here.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A lot of my friends, you know, went-- went to Israel.
Because they had no opportunity to come to America [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So-- after the-- my first yahrzeit, the first ever I went to shul, you know, to say Kaddish everyday.
I'd rewired a big synagogue, what that the-- the Germans ripped off the chandeliers, in Debrecen.
Yes the same shul.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brother and I took a train back to Budapest.
At-- at the first yahrzeit.
And we took the train to Szombathely, which is a larger city at the-- close to the border of Hungary.
My brother stayed there and made arrangements to have a grave opened up in a-- in a large Jewish cemetery, and see if we could find a rabbi.
There was-- I remember, there was a man with a beard.
Apparently he must have been the rabbi because there weren't-- there were some Jews left, but not many.
And I went to this place where my mother is buried.
And I hired local people to open up the grave.
And I went to the tar carpenter and paid him, and-- and-- and paid the midwife for being-- and thanked them for helping us, for being nice.
And-- and nobody would go down into the grave to put the rope under.
Of course, I went down.
I put the rope under the box.
And we picked her up and there was a doctor there, had to open the coffin.
And I couldn't avoid it, to look.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's what I live with the rest of my life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We took her back to-- I took her to Szombathely, where we had a-- a regular levaya, what a Jewish woman deserves.
And that's where she's resting.
And I've been back, in the past 50 years, three times.
They gave it to us.
And I hope to go again next year.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, I have a nephew, my sister's son, who is 35 years old.
[DRINKS] Lives in California.
He-- he travels to Europe.
And when I-- when his mother, I guess, told him the story, and I told him some of it, he wanted to go to see the grave.
And he went to Europe two years ago, and went to this town, the city, Szombathely, an American boy that don't speak English-- I mean, don't speak Hungarian.
The cemetery was closed.
And he climbed over a wall.
Took him an hour.
He found the grave.
He wanted to spend a little time with his grandmother, that he never had.
[CRYING] And I helped take my two daughters next year, to their grandmother's grave that they never knew.
[CRYING] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from there, we left Hungary.
And went to Prague.
And from Prague to Paris.
And from Paris to America.
[CRYING] But there isn't a day in my life that I don't think about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Or at night, when I go to sleep, when I can't fall asleep, because that comes to my mind.
And I ask why?
A righteous woman, a religious woman, an Orthodox woman, the way she had to die, at the age 42.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And she died when I most needed her.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
Yes.
Yes.
[CRYING]
Pardon me?
Very nice.
When we came on the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was a six-day trip.
That was the largest ocean-- ocean liner at that time.
Because it was converted to a troopship.
And that was what was left that way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The trip was pleasant.
Of course, excited to see my father, who I haven't seen since I was 12 years old, and here I'm 18, and six foot tall.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My brother and sister, they were seasick on the-- on the trip.
When we arrive to New York, the first thing I saw is the West Side Highway, or the Belt Parkway, with all the lights and cars.
It was during the night.
And the ship docked, took almost all day.
And we were able to get off.
I stood at the railing next to my brother and we saw my father at the dock a-- from a long distance.
And he hollered up to my brother, where is Arnold?
My brother said, he's standing next to me.
He didn't recognize me.
When was this?
1946, December.
I think December the 22nd.
And then finally, we walked off the ship.
You can imagine, four children jumping at one man's neck.
[SIGHS] He cried like a baby.
[SIGHS] And of course, we had a lot of cousins that waited for us.
My father lived with my grandmother that time, on Sumner Avenue, in Brooklyn.
And there were three or four cars that were waiting for us.
And we split up in different cars.
And we went to my grandmother's apartment.
My father had an affordable apartment fixed up for us on Kosciuszko Street, just in the middle of the block.
Coming across the bridge, the Brooklyn Bridge, what the first bridge, what I saw.
One of my cousins asked me, you know, how do you like America?
I says, I haven't seen nothing yet.
And it's dark.
It's winter.
There was a big dinner at my grandmother's.
Now, this grandmother is my mother's mother that came here many years ago, with her sister and her-- and her son.
And of course, there was a full house with all the relatives, the cousins that I knew from Europe that came out before the water, some my age, some older.
It was pleasant.
And at the same token, it was sad because somebody was missing.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The wound was very fresh.
Still is.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] After that evening, we went over to the apartment and unpacked ourselves.
I couldn't wait for the daylight to come down in the morning and see what America looks like.
Well, I'm sorry to say, I wasn't too impressed.
It was filthy, the streets, the garbage, the garbage cans for all the house.
And I was unhappy.
I went upstairs and I cried where nobody saw me, you know?
Why didn't I go to Israel?
Although on the other hand, I was happy that I was with my father.
And now I believe it's the greatest country in the world.
And I love it.
Not really.
There were some-- you know, you know, Europeans get together.
We talk about the Holocaust, the-- the experiences, the things that happened.
I was asked, one time, to speak to-- in my shul we had a Hebrew school, to speak to the Hebrew school children.
I couldn't get myself to do it.
This is pretty difficult too.
My niece, my brother's daughter asked me a few years ago that I should make a tape recording of what happened.
She would like to have one.
I bought the tape recorder.
It's still upstairs.
I started, and I just put it down.
I couldn't do it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, the life I started in the United States, of course I had a trade.
I was an electrician.
I was very handy and knowledgeable.
And I had a-- an uncle that got me a job by an electrician, for $25 a week to rewind motors, bend out motors, electric motors.
And I worked for him for about two-- two or three months.
And my first job was to-- in the cold winter of 1947-- he had several pickup trucks, to check the weather in the morning and gas it up.
And so that his mechanics that are making $100 a day-- I mean, $100 a week can go out to do jobs.
And then he had me in a basement next to a guy that was earning 120 a week, doing the same work that I was doing.
And me, he only paid $25.
At that time, copper was a nickel.
And I traveled to Flushing, to-- to work.
I asked him for a little more money.
I said, I'm doing the same work as the guy next to me.
And he called me-- he says, you're a greenhorn.
Well, I didn't take it politely.
And I sort of smacked them when they called me there.
And he fired me.
He told my uncle they're having me arrested for assault.
My uncle says, you arrest him, you'll never get another job from me.
So that was it.
I left.
I got into the building trade, physical work, roofing, at $60 a week.
And within six months, I was a foreman.
Within six months, I owned an automobile.
I bought a car.
Of course, my father lent me $300.
And I paid him back.
I didn't have a license yet, and I drove a truck, you know, for the contractor.
Until I-- I couldn't speak English well.
And I failed the test.
Because I didn't-- I didn't understand the questions.
But after three times, I passed it.
The drive-- road test, I passed no problem.
The English, that was the problem.
I worked with black people.
And I learned the language first.
From then, it was uphill.
I've done-- I worked for this company.
I've done large work, large jobs.

Like, for instance, the year that I first year I got married that my wife is expecting, I managed the job at the Morgan Annex Post Office, which was the largest post office in New York, whole square block.
Worked with union people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, the outside elevator we erected, you know, to carry the material up.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --I worked for that company six years.
Then my brother-in-law was in a similar business.
He asked me if I want to come work for him.
And I worked for him a couple of years.
And the-- in the shul that I went to with my father, there was a man that had a son come to America, in 1947.
It was a [? bleche, ?] a metal man from Europe.
He was almost a couple of years older than I am, if I could get him a job.
And I said, OK, I'll find out and I'll let you know.
I got him a job and we became friends.
We used to see each other occasionally.
And he went into business for himself after a couple of years.
And he kept telling me if you ever look for-- look for work, look for a job, come and see me.
So I broke out with my brother.
We had a little partnership.
And I went to see him.
And I stayed with him 34 years.
I was a partner for 5 out of the 34.
And I sold back my partnership.
I raised two beautiful daughters.
And I have three grandchildren, two grandsons and a granddaughter.
They--
My daughter?
Oh, the daughter is Susan, Susan Miller is her married name.
My younger daughter is Ruth Col, C-O-L.
And they own beautiful homes in New Jersey, very independent.
My daughter, my older daughter is a teacher, a schoolteacher.
My younger daughter is an executive for a company that monitors the oil companies.
She's graduating-- she goes to college at night.
The company is sending her.
She is graduating with a bachelor's degree this-- this coming spring.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My older grandson is Evan.
God bless him, he's six foot two.
His brother is six foot.
He's-- he's 17.
And my granddaughter is 17.
She goes to the high school.
She's graduating, going to go to college next year.
And she works part time at the Freehold Mall.
She works part time--
Her name is Lauren, Lauren Col-- no, Lauren Weisler, is from the second marriage.
My wife name-- I met her 48 years ago.
We-- I met her amongst the [?
Greens, ?] that we used to hang out together through friends.
Her name is Betty Greenbaum, maiden name.
She's done an excellent job raising my children, our children, I should say.
She's active for the past 30 years in my synagogue, Always to raise funds, to raise money.
I'm active also, the past 18 years, 20 years, since my father passed away.
I was a three-times-a-year shul goer.
But since he passed away, I go every Shabbos.
And I had a beautiful Sefar Torah in the shul, under his name and my wife's parents' name, in their honor, which I see every Shabbos, and I kiss it and they learn from it every Shabbos.
To me, that's a great honor that I did for them.
What effect did it have?
Life is cheap.
I been so close to death so many times that nothing scares me.
I just hope and pray for the well being of my kids and my grandchildren.
Oh yes.
That affected it very bad.
Not now, anymore.
But when-- when we came here, well, after my-- I buried my mother, I-- like I said, I said Kaddish for a year.
Yeah, OK.
